channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award
item.description:
Curator's Take: This recognition of Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard with computing's most prestigious award underscores how foundational their quantum cryptography work has become to both quantum physics and computer science. The duo's pioneering BB84 protocol from 1984 essentially launched the field of quantum cryptography by demonstrating how quantum mechanics could guarantee absolutely secure communication—a breakthrough that seemed purely theoretical at the time but now underpins commercial quantum key distribution systems protecting sensitive data worldwide. Their win marks a significant moment where the quantum computing revolution is being acknowledged at the highest levels of the computing establishment, validating decades of research that bridges fundamental physics with practical information security. This award also highlights how quantum technologies are increasingly viewed not as exotic physics experiments but as transformative computing paradigms worthy of the same recognition given to classical computing pioneers.

Duo bag award often described as the “Nobel Prize in Computing” The post Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem
item.description:
Curator's Take: Denmark's quantum ecosystem is emerging as a compelling case study for how strategic policy and international talent attraction can rapidly accelerate a nation's quantum capabilities. This development reflects the increasingly global nature of quantum research, where countries are recognizing that success depends not just on domestic investment but on creating environments that draw top international scientists and foster cross-border collaboration. The focus on Denmark is particularly noteworthy given the country's strong foundation in quantum physics through institutions like the Niels Bohr Institute, suggesting that historical scientific excellence combined with modern policy initiatives can create powerful momentum in the quantum race. This trend of nations actively courting quantum talent mirrors similar efforts in the US, UK, and other quantum-ambitious countries, highlighting how the field is becoming a key battleground for technological and economic leadership.

Ambition and international talent converge as Denmark scales up in quantum science The post International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in metasurface technology could dramatically accelerate the path to large-scale neutral atom quantum computers by potentially trapping over 100,000 atoms on a single device. Neutral atom systems are emerging as serious contenders for fault-tolerant quantum computing because they offer excellent qubit connectivity and can be precisely controlled with optical tweezers, but scaling to the massive qubit counts needed for practical applications has remained a major engineering challenge. The ability to generate such unprecedented numbers of trapped atoms from a single metasurface represents a significant step toward the million-qubit systems that many experts believe will be necessary for solving real-world problems like drug discovery and materials science. This development positions neutral atom platforms to compete more directly with superconducting and trapped ion approaches in the race to build commercially viable quantum computers.

Technique boosts prospects for building quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits The post Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computing meets the Möbius molecule
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating convergence where quantum computing is being applied to understand exotic molecular structures that classical computers struggle to simulate accurately. The Möbius molecule represents a particularly challenging test case for quantum algorithms due to its complex electronic structure and topological properties, making it an ideal benchmark for demonstrating quantum advantage in chemistry applications. What's especially exciting is that researchers believe current quantum hardware is finally reaching the threshold where such complex molecular systems become computationally accessible, potentially opening new avenues for drug discovery and materials science. This represents a practical step toward the long-promised quantum advantage in computational chemistry, moving beyond toy problems to molecules with real-world relevance.

A complex structure with multiple electrons is within reach of today's hardware.

Read original at Ars Technica - Science
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IonQ | IonQ & University of Cambridge Launch Quantum Innovation Centre
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant milestone for quantum computing accessibility in Europe, as IonQ's 256-qubit trapped-ion system will provide Cambridge researchers with hands-on access to one of the most advanced quantum computers currently available. The collaboration is particularly noteworthy because it combines IonQ's industry-leading quantum hardware with Cambridge's world-class quantum research expertise, potentially accelerating breakthroughs in quantum algorithms, error correction, and applications across multiple disciplines. By establishing this Innovation Centre, the partnership creates a crucial bridge between academic quantum research and practical quantum computing development, positioning the UK as a major player in the global quantum race. The 256-qubit scale puts this system well into the range where quantum advantage becomes possible for certain problems, making it an invaluable resource for pushing the boundaries of what's computationally feasible with today's quantum technology.

IonQ partners with the University of Cambridge to deploy a 256-qubit quantum system and launch a new Innovation Centre to accelerate UK quantum research.

Read original at IonQ Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating discovery where researchers have identified a new type of quantum advantage in determining the parity of permutations, adding to the growing catalog of tasks where quantum systems outperform classical computers. What makes this particularly intriguing is that it was unexpected - these kinds of surprising quantum advantages often reveal deeper connections between quantum mechanics and computational complexity that weren't previously understood. The finding contributes to our fundamental understanding of where the boundary lies between classical and quantum computational power, which is crucial for identifying the most promising applications for near-term quantum devices. While this specific permutation parity task may seem abstract, such theoretical breakthroughs often lay the groundwork for discovering practical quantum algorithms in cryptography, optimization, and other fields.

The list of things that quantum features enable us to do just got a little longer The post Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates quantum memories enabling single-photon interferometry across 1.5 kilometers, a significant step toward quantum-enhanced astronomical observations that could revolutionize how we study distant celestial objects. Long-baseline interferometry typically requires incredibly precise synchronization between widely separated telescopes, but quantum memories could store and synchronize single photons from faint astronomical sources, potentially allowing telescopes separated by thousands of kilometers to work together as a single, massive virtual instrument. The ability to preserve quantum coherence over these distances opens the door to unprecedented angular resolution in space-based observations, which could help detect exoplanets and probe fundamental physics in extreme cosmic environments. While still in early stages, this work bridges quantum information science with practical astronomical applications in a way that could transform both fields.

Single-photon interferometry achieved over 1.5 km The post Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in reading out Majorana qubits represents a crucial step toward realizing one of quantum computing's most promising but elusive platforms. Majorana qubits are theoretically protected from many types of noise that plague conventional qubits, potentially offering a pathway to more stable quantum computers, but actually demonstrating and controlling them has proven extraordinarily challenging. While the ability to read out these exotic quasiparticles marks significant experimental progress, the scalability concerns mentioned highlight the ongoing gap between laboratory demonstrations and practical quantum systems. This work adds to the growing body of evidence that topological quantum computing, while still years away from practical implementation, remains a viable long-term approach to fault-tolerant quantum computation.

Mechanism could pave the way for more robust quantum computation, but questions remain over scalability The post Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Building advanced quantum chemistry calculations with Kvantify Qrunch on Amazon Braket
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article showcases a practical milestone in making quantum chemistry accessible to researchers today, rather than waiting for fault-tolerant quantum computers. Kvantify Qrunch's integration with Amazon Braket represents the growing maturity of quantum cloud platforms, offering chemists and materials scientists user-friendly tools to explore molecular simulations on real NISQ hardware. The focus on hybrid algorithms is particularly significant because it bridges the gap between classical and quantum computing, allowing researchers to extract meaningful results from current noisy devices while building expertise for future breakthroughs. This democratization of quantum chemistry tools could accelerate drug discovery, catalyst design, and materials research across multiple industries.

Quantum computing promises breakthroughs in chemistry simulations. However, building scalable calculations on today’s noisy quantum hardware remains challenging. This blog introduces Kvantify Qrunch software on Amazon Braket. It enables advanced chemistry calculations using efficient hybrid quantum algorithms on current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices. Chemistry is at the heart of several industries with profound economic […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial milestone in scaling quantum cryptography from laboratory demonstrations to real-world metropolitan networks. Device-independent quantum key distribution over 100 km distances means that quantum-encrypted communications can now potentially secure entire cities without relying on trusted hardware components, addressing one of the most significant vulnerabilities in current quantum cryptographic systems. The achievement demonstrates that quantum-secure internet infrastructure is transitioning from proof-of-concept to practical deployment, bringing us closer to networks that could withstand attacks from future quantum computers. This citywide scale represents the sweet spot between current fiber-optic infrastructure and the coverage needed for meaningful quantum-secured communications in urban environments.

Device-independent quantum-encrypted keys distributed over 100 km The post Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Physics World podcast featuring Bert de Jong offers valuable insights into the Quantum Systems Accelerator, a major DOE-funded initiative that's working to bridge the gap between quantum research and practical computing applications. As one of the key figures at Lawrence Berkeley National Lab, de Jong provides an insider's perspective on how national laboratories are coordinating efforts to develop the hardware, software, and algorithmic advances needed to make quantum computing truly useful. The QSA represents a significant policy shift toward more coordinated, application-focused quantum development rather than isolated research efforts. For anyone trying to understand how quantum computing is moving from academic curiosity to engineered reality, this conversation illuminates the strategic thinking behind America's quantum infrastructure investments.

Bert de Jong of Lawrence Berkeley National Lab is our podcast guest The post Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-Guided Cluster Algorithms for Combinatorial Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This AWS research introduces quantum-guided cluster algorithms that cleverly combine classical and quantum approaches to tackle notoriously difficult combinatorial optimization problems like scheduling and routing. The hybrid methodology is particularly significant because it addresses a key limitation of current quantum hardware by using quantum insights to guide classical algorithms, potentially delivering practical benefits before we achieve full-scale fault-tolerant quantum computers. What makes this work especially noteworthy is its focus on real-world applications across industries, from logistics to finance, where even modest improvements in optimization could translate to substantial economic value. The collaborative effort from AWS's quantum team signals growing enterprise investment in near-term quantum algorithms that bridge the gap between today's noisy quantum devices and tomorrow's quantum advantage.

This post was contributed by Peter Eder, Aron Kerschbaumer, Jernej Rudi Finžgar, Raimel Medina, Martin Schuetz, Helmut Katzgraber, Sarah Braun, and Christian Mendl. Optimizing complex systems is a major challenge in science and industry. Many tasks – such as scheduling, routing, portfolio selection, or network design – can be written as combinatorial optimization problems that […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores a fascinating connection between quantum entanglement and computational complexity theory, suggesting that the amount of entanglement in a quantum system can serve as a measure of how difficult a computational problem is to solve. This represents a significant theoretical breakthrough that could help researchers better understand the fundamental limits of both classical and quantum computing, potentially providing new tools for classifying which problems quantum computers can solve exponentially faster than classical ones. The work bridges two major areas of quantum research - quantum information theory and computational complexity - offering fresh insights into why certain problems are inherently hard and how quantum effects like entanglement might be the key to understanding computational difficulty. This could ultimately guide the development of more efficient quantum algorithms and help identify new applications where quantum computers offer genuine advantages.

Efficiency and hardness of a computation problem are revealed The post Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Reservoir computing on an analog Rydberg-atom quantum computer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article demonstrates a fascinating convergence of quantum computing and machine learning, showing how Rydberg-atom quantum computers can serve as powerful "reservoirs" for processing complex data patterns without the need for extensive training algorithms. What makes this particularly compelling is the focus on pharmaceutical applications where small datasets are common—a notorious challenge in classical machine learning where models often struggle with limited training data. The researchers' success with both image classification and time series prediction suggests that quantum reservoir computing could offer genuine advantages in specialized domains where data is precious and every sample counts. This work represents a practical step toward quantum machine learning applications that could deliver real-world benefits in the near term, rather than waiting for fault-tolerant quantum computers.

This post shows how quantum reservoir computing (QRC) can tackle machine learning challenges using Rydberg-atom quantum computers. Readers will learn how QRC works, see its performance on image classification and time series prediction tasks, and understand when it outperforms classical methods—particularly for small datasets in pharmaceutical research. After decades of progress, machine learning (ML) has […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in metasurface-assisted atom trapping represents a major leap toward truly large-scale quantum computers, with researchers demonstrating a path to neutral atom arrays exceeding 100,000 qubits - orders of magnitude beyond current systems. Neutral atom platforms have emerged as one of the most promising quantum computing architectures due to their scalability and flexibility, but creating and controlling massive arrays has remained a significant technical challenge. The metasurface approach offers a potentially game-changing solution by using engineered optical surfaces to precisely position and manipulate thousands of atoms simultaneously, potentially accelerating the timeline for fault-tolerant quantum computers that require millions of physical qubits. This development positions neutral atom systems as serious contenders in the race to build practical quantum computers capable of solving real-world problems.

Technique lays the groundwork for neutral-atom quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits, say physicists The post Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial but often overlooked aspect of quantum technology development: the need for standardized measurement protocols that will enable global interoperability. As quantum sensors achieve unprecedented precision in measuring everything from gravitational waves to magnetic fields, establishing common international standards becomes essential for comparing results across different labs and transitioning these technologies from research to commercial applications. The collaboration between National Metrology Institutes like NPL represents a foundational step toward ensuring that quantum sensing breakthroughs can be validated, reproduced, and ultimately trusted by industries ranging from healthcare to navigation. While quantum sensing often gets less attention than quantum computing, this standards work could prove equally important for bringing practical quantum advantages to everyday technologies.

NPL has joined forces with other leading National Metrology Institutes to shape the international standards effort in quantum technologies The post Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers?
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development from Silicon Quantum Computing represents a significant milestone in the quest to build quantum computers using silicon, the same material that powers today's classical computers. While 11 qubits may seem modest compared to superconducting systems with hundreds of qubits, silicon-based quantum processors offer compelling advantages including potentially better scalability, longer coherence times, and the ability to leverage existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure. The fact that this Australian spin-out has achieved what they claim is "modality-leading" performance suggests silicon quantum computing is maturing from a promising research direction into a viable commercial path. If silicon qubits can maintain their advantages while scaling up, this could dramatically accelerate quantum computing adoption by tapping into the trillion-dollar semiconductor industry's established supply chains and expertise.

Australian spin-out Silicon Quantum Computing makes the case with a modality-leading 11-qubit processor The post Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers? appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough challenges one of quantum mechanics' most fundamental principles - the no-cloning theorem - by demonstrating that encrypted quantum states can indeed be duplicated and distributed across multiple locations. The research represents a significant step toward building a robust quantum internet infrastructure, where quantum information could be backed up and transmitted more reliably than previously thought possible. While the qubits must remain encrypted during the cloning process, this "elegant" workaround opens new possibilities for quantum error correction, distributed quantum computing, and secure quantum communication networks that could revolutionize how we process and share sensitive information globally.

“Elegant” result has implications for a quantum internet The post Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals Microsoft's continued push to make quantum computing more accessible to mainstream developers, a crucial step toward bridging the gap between today's experimental quantum systems and tomorrow's practical applications. By expanding their developer toolset, Microsoft is positioning Azure Quantum to compete more directly with IBM's Qiskit and Google's Cirq in the race to create the dominant quantum software ecosystem. The focus on versatility suggests they're addressing one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: the need for flexible tools that can work across different quantum hardware architectures and programming paradigms. As quantum hardware continues to mature, having robust, user-friendly development platforms will likely determine which companies can successfully onboard the next wave of quantum programmers and researchers.

Microsoft is committed to providing the tools that developers and scientists need to enable applications on quantum machines. The post Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform appeared first on Microsoft Azure Quantum Blog .

Read original at Microsoft Quantum Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a fascinating new perspective on one of quantum mechanics' most puzzling aspects by treating wavefunction collapse as a continuous process rather than an instantaneous event. The quantum state diffusion framework described here offers a fresh mathematical lens for understanding measurement, characterizing it through entropy production in a way that could bridge the gap between quantum theory and thermodynamics. This approach is particularly relevant for quantum sensing applications, where understanding the precise dynamics of measurement could lead to more sensitive detectors and better control over quantum information extraction. While the work is primarily theoretical, it addresses fundamental questions about the measurement problem that have implications for how we design and optimize quantum devices across computing, sensing, and communication platforms.

Quantum state diffusion framework makes it possible to characterize quantum measurement in terms of entropy production The post Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly
